speaker: interviewer
text: And can you describe that further?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I know that this Polish gentleman or boy, he-- he was not very, very old either. He-- he worked there too, as an electrician, so I think. He had his eyes on my boots. And every time he turn around and-- he spoke only Polish, so he needed to get an interpreter to try to talk to me in German.

speaker: interviewer
text: And somebody came up, I think the kapo or whoever-- you know what a kapo is-- that he would like to get those boots, and will be ready to give me back another pair of shoes so I wouldn't be without shoes. But he also bring some [NON-ENGLISH]. You know what [NON-ENGLISH] is? Bacon and bread and something to eat.

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, well, OK. I mean, he-- he wouldn't stop anyhow. As long as he seen those boots, he'll keep on going, trying to get them, and maybe bring me a little more bacon. I mean, the bacon there is not like they have it here. It's a bacon you-- it's a big piece, like this. And, you know, you slice pieces off and off, OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: I said, OK. I'll do it. And I split the thing with another-- maybe with the kapo who helped me do that, you understand? So he-- so I-- I had for a few days, I had a good meal for a few days. I felt OK. What was your normal meal?

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, the normal meal? Well, let me tell you. In the morning, you got out. You-- you always kept your bowl. A bowl what was a-- a metal bowl and had a-- a-- the inside was lined with-- not plastic. It's-- it's a-- it's a-- how should I say? It's a-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] something what-- what it could break, and it falls in pieces. It's-- it's not metal. It's like, you have-- anyhow, it's insulation also. That bowl-- without that bowl, you wouldn't have no soup. You wouldn't have nothing. So that bowl was your-- your very, very, very only thing you-- you would be able to have some food. If you wouldn't have the bowl, you couldn't go there with your hands open and put a soup in there. [LAUGHS] And that was not-- that was not possible. So that they would tell you, well, sorry. Next.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that what-- what-- what we talked about, having food. In the morning, you got up. You get-- in that bowl, you got tea. No coffee. Well, tea. It was watery tea. A piece of bread. OK? Cornbread. And sometimes cheese, maybe, or sometimes a piece of salami. That was the morning.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then you came home at night. The first thing, take your shirt off. Somebody-- and turn it upside down-- somebody had to look if you have like lice. That was a very important thing for the SS. There were big signs in the barracks. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH] What they said? [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH] One lice is your death. Because lice are carrying disease. Even the SS was afraid they could contract too when they're together with us. So that-- that was-- and at night, you came home. You got a soup sometimes. It was made from-- one day in a week you've got diet. They called it [NON-ENGLISH]. The [NON-ENGLISH] was corn flakes, white, and might be a little milk in it. That was the best one.

speaker: interviewee
text: And [CHUCKLES] people used to, you know, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] go and-- and exchange it for something else. Well, I'll give you a piece of-- I give you [NON-ENGLISH], a [NON-ENGLISH]. You got me-- give me a cigarette, or give me this, or give me that. I've seen that people-- what I have-- I have never forgotten one man who came there. He was from Berlin, a Jewish fellow. Little guy. One time in the morning, I seen him giving away his ration, his food ration. First food ration in the morning, away for a cigarette. But after three, four weeks, he was skinny. Just bones. That's all. And he-- later, he ended up in the chimney. Another thing I haven't forgotten, there was a fellow, his name was Stein. Paul Stein. He came with us in the same barracks. And Paul Stein was one of our madrichim, our leaders. From the [NON-ENGLISH]. And one night, you heard shots and somebody scream, down! Down! Go down! Lay on the floor! That's what the-- the guards who were all Ukrainians, Ukrainians in SS uniform-- they ran away from Stalin, and the SS took them. They-- they weren't-- they were not good enough to put them to the front, naturally. They probably wouldn't go. When the Russians would have seen them, they'll kill them right away.

speaker: interviewer
text: So Paul Stein was the first one who committed suicide. And I-- in the morning, I got out, and I see him laying in the-- in the-- next to the-- to the-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the-- no. [NON-ENGLISH], how would I call that-- the electric-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] oh, the electric-- The fence?

speaker: interviewee
text: Fence. There you go. The electric fence. There was one first one was only that high, and there were signs on it. Electric fence. Stay away. And then comes-- came the other one, another one. And then came-- come the big one, fence. The big one. You know, with half round and full of-- full of--

speaker: interviewer
text: The barbed wire?

speaker: interviewee
text: Barbed wire. And the guards were sitting up on top. And If you just got close to it, even the first one, they pulled their gun up and called to you. Go back, or else. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] But Paul Stein. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, he knew he wouldn't get-- he wouldn't get out of it anyhow. So might as well. It was fast. Getting-- getting go through death. There's a lot of-- you know, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] what is it called? Humiliation. You know? No food. No this. And kicking here and kicking there. So. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] OK. How long were you in Auschwitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Auschwitz? OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: In that camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was in this Buna. There was one more episode, I would like to bring it out.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us.

speaker: interviewee
text: I came to-- I had one kommando I was assigned to, and that was unloading cement out of a boxcar. Cement was a 500 kilo, pound. OK? They had a plank going up one way, and a plank going up the other way. And the kapo stood alongside of it, and two men were in the box car. And they told us, run. [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]. Run up one plank, put your-- your head down and your-- your-- your-- your body. And then they'll-- they'll put that cement down. And then [NON-ENGLISH]! Down! Running out. And then they picked up that-- that-- that 500 kilo pound cement and took it away and piled it up.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that was going all day. I said, damn. I'm never going to make that. No way. So what I did-- there was lots of people. Lots of electric welding going on there, OK? So I don't know how I got that idea. What I knew from being a-- a machinist that arc welding, don't look into it. It will burn your retina. So I looked into it. I wanted to be sick. I wanted to be sick, so the next day, I wouldn't be able to go back to this one. I might have ended up somewhere else, a better one. And that's what happened. I looked in the arc welding for how long? Half a minute, a minute. That's enough. And I felt right after that that somebody is like, taking sand and throw it in your eyes. And as I come home, I already-- so they told me, oh, you-- you can't see.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, go-- go to the infirmary. It's-- you know, it's a little thing. And here I met a man who-- we were also together from [NON-ENGLISH], Ernst Mitchell. He, today, is the head of the UGA in New York. He wrote-- written a book. And I don't know, he might-- he'd written a book. It's called [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Ernst. I talked to him a few days ago. So Ernest said, Manfred, it's OK. You're here. You're going to be-- we're going to be OK tomorrow morning. Don't stay here. The SS comes here. You know, they know everybody who's in here is sick anyhow. So get out. Don't stay here.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I got out. And when I got out, they assigned me to another company, another kommando. So I was saved out of that. [CHUCKLES] And later, I-- I-- I met a fellow, he was a kapo. And he-- he-- I don't know.

speaker: interviewee
text: I stood there one day. Oh, I felt by myself. I started crying. And-- and I said, well, well, that's the end. I don't know who to turn to, what to do. So his name was Harry. Harry Naphtaniel. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And Harry comes, and he puts one-- touches me on the shoulder. He said, what's wrong? I said, well, nothing is wrong. I don't know. I don't think I'm going to make this. I'm going to-- it's the end of me. He said, ah, no worry. Come with me. We'll go in this barrack, and I'll take you in my kommando. And you wouldn't have to work that hard. Don't worry. So he did. And he-- after a while, we were put into-- into another camp. It's a-- somebody-- the SS come up, and he said, we are looking for roofers. So Harry bumped me and said, say yes. You are a roofer. You know everything about roofing. That's what I said. So they took me, all of us, and sent me to-- I think it was Sosnowitz. It was a small town called. And there was about four or five barracks. We worked on the barracks, put the roofs on, and so on. And after that, we had to go to the factory. I don't know anything. What factory? Factory who made the biggest and the best German gun they ever had in World War I. Anybody who listens to this who was in World War-- in World War II, not I. Sorry-- knows about the 88 millimeter anti-aircraft gun.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's a-- a self-propellant gun. So I worked on this gun. It was about, oh, I don't know, oh, from-- from the wall up to here long. And overhead cranes came in, put it on your machine, and you had to lock it down here and there. And then you worked on it. Everything was ready. They had [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] how far you can go and how far you cannot go. How far you can-- you can take so much off, and how far you cannot. So you had to say within the limit, within the tolerance, they called it. Well, one time, I forgot to lock a thing down. And the-- the-- the barrel started throwing-- throwing around. And I had my fingers in there too, on the side. And I cut my hands. Blood was running.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I shut it off as fast as I could. And here comes the soldiers. It was not SS. There was only one SS, who was the-- what's his name? That was [NON-ENGLISH]. That was the unterscharfuhrer. He was the head of us. But the Wehrmacht, the soldiers, they were professionals who worked on this-- on this particular gun. First thing, they come up and say, sabotage, huh? I said, please, no. It's not sabotage. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I-- I cut myself too. I forgot to lock it down. OK. They-- they believed me, and they took the thing away, and they gave me another one. That means they-- they trusted me to do this.

speaker: interviewee
text: But the Germans were absolutely incredible, way ahead in their technology, how to do this, and how to make those shells. The shells were done next-- in-- in the next apartment, in a-- all right, already with automatic machines. Those days. In '42. And they had-- they had prisoners from Thessaloniki that came from Greece, and they had them work on the oven to heat the-- the shells. They came in-- in pieces like this and steel. They took them, because they felt, oh, they're used to the heat in Greece. So they can take that heat. The poor guys. I talked to a doctor once who said they probably were nothing but selling clothes and doing that. You know. But they worked there. And they were skinny fellows. And-- and-- and they-- they did their job.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this was your time in Sosnowitz.

speaker: interviewee
text: In Sosnowitz, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did you go from there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Then winter came. It was '44 already, and then, all of a sudden, we were called [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] out, back in the barracks, and put your-- put ever what you have. Put it on. We had a-- we had some-- some-- they're called-- well, suits. Regular suits and a-- and a coat. Well, a thin coat, whatever it was. And we started marching. First thing was Katowice. We ended up in the-- in the slaughterhouse and on the floor. Everybody was--

speaker: interviewer
text: To stay there.

speaker: interviewee
text: We stayed there overnight, yeah. Then, next day, the things started. Outside was snowing, cold.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you're marching again.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, marching again. And then we came to night. They looked for a place where there was a ravine. You know, somehow they had-- they had pushed us down in here. And the guards was standing up on top.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what-- what could you do? I know, in order to keep warm, we laid there like sardines, one next to the other. Not to-- not to get cold. And-- and you got up the next morning, and there was dozens of them, dead. Who-- who just couldn't get up. They were dead. They just died right there. So they-- they assumed a-- a death squad. You know, they are left behind. They had to dig holes and throw them in, so on. That was like, we call it a death march. We finally ended up not far from the Czechoslovakian border.

speaker: interviewee
text: And here, we-- they put us again into boxcars. And after about a day's ride, or a day and a half, whatever it took, we ended up in Mauthausen. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] That was Austria. One of the-- the biggest they had in Austria. And I can remember, we came in there, two had to take a shower, do this, and you know. Be en-liced, be-- be cleaned up.

speaker: interviewer
text: Deloused.

speaker: interviewee
text: Deloused, yeah. And-- and it's-- that's German. Deloused, yeah. And one thing I can remember, there was a a fellow, a Jewish fellow from Holland on a-- on a stretcher. And he-- he must have broken his leg or something. He was laying there, and he was begging everybody, please, help me. Help.

speaker: interviewer
text: What could you do? I mean, it rings in my ears still, how desperate the man was. He knew that he's not going to be able to go-- stay alive. That they were taking him and killing him and get rid of him. There was no other thing.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then comes something I will not forget. We marched. After we cleaned up, we marched up towards the camp. And here, all of a sudden, somebody-- could have been SS or a kapo or what, we need people to work in the crematorium. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] What, since Auschwitz always was a good job, it means food. And nobody bothers you. You're, so to speak, on your own. But there's one thing. I think, in Auschwitz, what they did, after so long, they took those people and got rid of them because they were witnesses. And I-- I wasn't putting my hand up. And another fellow, a Jewish fellow from Poland, also put their hand up. Oh, he was-- oh. Then something went through my head, and I said, never, ever volunteer to do anything. Don't. So I didn't.

speaker: interviewer
text: When-- in order to make this story short-- when I came, after the war, I was liberated. I ended up in-- in-- in-- in Italy. Some fellow tapped me on the shoulder. I turn around. And I said, yeah? What is it? He said, you remember? We were together in Mauthausen. You remember the man-- or the kid-- who volunteered to go to the crematorium? I said yes, I can remember. I-- I just didn't want to do it. He says, you know, the Russians came. You know what they did with him? They took him alive and threw him in. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: One day, also, we got new shoes [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in that camp. And I had been walking, and something bothers me, you know, like you have a nail sticking into you in your heel in the back. I sit on the bed, and all of a sudden the heel comes off. I look at it. There is a piece, a gold piece, a Czech gold piece in there, in the heel, [CHUCKLES] a diamond ring. And a-- a-- I think an America silver-- gold dollar. I showed it to Harry. You know. Here, look. [GASPS] Harry was all, oh my god. You know, it was from a-- you know from where, from another Jew who was gassed. And he took his shoes, and he-- he put that, tried to-- to-- to live on that. And he went right away and took this, brought me back another pair of shoes, and then he went to the-- to the kitchen, wherever, and showed.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they gave him bread. They have him a-- a salami that long, bacon, and all kinds of stuff. Now, how are we going to save this stuff? Where-- where are we going to put it? But we-- All we did, we could do, we put it under our pillows in the back. Sleep on it so nobody will steal it again. Well, it was good for a few days. We ate, we give away a little here and there. And-- and here we went. Then I ended up-- from there, I ended up in Wien Meidling. Another long-- we want to go on? How did you get there?

speaker: interviewee
text: By train. They took us from there. They put us to Wien Meidling. There, we worked. As I found out a few days ago, it was the first German jet was built by Messerschmidt. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We didn't know what it was. I didn't know either. There was no-- there was an engine in the back, a hole in the back. There was no propeller. And the-- the-- the were put on outside. It was down in the ground. It was a-- it was a cave. Used to be a cave. And what--

speaker: interviewer
text: And this is where you worked.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Where I worked, yeah. And I also got my first lesson in being-- being whipped. You know, the-- we had to have a-- a-- a-- a little piece of cloth what was showing what is a-- it's one triangle. It's red. And the opposite triangle is yellow, what shows you that you are Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: I didn't have no sew. I mean-- didn't have needle and anything. And that thing looked like it's only red. So I came down, and I worked there. And the guy, the kapo, looked at me. He said, what are you? Aren't you Jew? Yes, I am. How come you have this stupid thing that shows that you are not, that you are a German, that you are a political prisoner? I said, I'm sorry. I can't-- I couldn't find a needle, and I couldn't find anything, you know, to do it right. So he said, OK. I'll get you 25 on your back.

speaker: interviewee
text: And one held my head, and he or somebody started hitting me, you know, with a whip. And he told me, if you stop, I'll start all over again. 25 lashes. OK. I got that. All right. I took it. I came home, I mean, home. I came out, and before I got into the barracks, here comes the head of the barracks and the kapo from there. Oh, yeah, yeah, that's that Jew. He did that. Oh, come on, buddy. We'll get you another one. So they gave me another 25 lashes. Already on the old-- what is already-- you know, your-- your bottom is sore and everything. And I-- I just passed out. I ended up in the shower with cold water running on me. And then they took me and threw me on my bed. And I was laying on my stomach for weeks till it healed up. All of a sudden my pants used to fit. They didn't fit before. It was so swollen. [CHUCKLES] That was Mauthausen. That was, I mean, Vienna. And then back to Mauthausen.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that was in Wien Meidling. That's near Vienna.

speaker: interviewee
text: Wien Meidling. Yeah, that's near Meidling. Near Vienna. Wien Meidling.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you there, do you believe?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not too long. We were not very long, because that was towards the end of the war already. The Russians were outside. You could hear-- you could hear the-- the-- the shots. You could hear the-- the-- no. What is called? Cannons. Cannons shooting, and things like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you get from that camp--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, from there--

speaker: interviewer
text: --back to Mauthausen?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember now how. I think we-- we took a train. Wouldn't be too far to walk, or they wanted us getting out fast back to Mauthausen. And then, from there, there was a lot of-- a lot of Hungarian Jews were added to that camp. It was right close to the [INAUDIBLE]. And they came from Hungary, and they-- they were in civilian clothes. They didn't have time anymore to put them-- the pajamas on. They didn't have any.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we slept in-- they had big tents. They opened up tents and let us sleep on the floor in the tents. And some of them, from those people, those-- those Hungarian Jews, they dug holes in the ground, like you know, like soldiers do in the war. Like foxholes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Foxholes. Right. And then they slept in there. And then the SS come and said, you get out there, or we get you out. So I guess they didn't want to listen. And it-- it didn't take long. A couple of German jets-- not jets, but fighter came with machine guns and sprayed with machine guns and killed them. Just as they were.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then a day later or so, people come running around. You want to have piece of meat? Have a piece of meat. Look at it. You want to buy that?

speaker: interviewee
text: You know what that was. That was from the bodies. They cut off. They cut off. And gave them, trying to sell them. You should eat it. I just looked at it. I said, well, it cannot be. It's impossible. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Well, we stood there not too long, slept in a-- and slept in the tents.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is the end of tape four, interview with Fred--
